title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a museum education officer called Alice talking about opportunities for volunteers. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a museum education officer called Alice talking about opportunities for volunteers. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: A museum education officer called Alice introduces a volunteer scheme.
extracts.audio_script: Hello. I’m Alice, the education officer at Barton Town Museum, and I would like to tell you how volunteers can become involved. Our museum is in a rather unusual building. Some visitors remember it as a bank, because there is still a safe downstairs, but its original purpose was as the town’s [9]post office[/9]. It opened as a museum twenty-five years ago and now receives thousands of visitors annually. We display local objects, photographs and recorded stories, and our volunteers make it possible for us to offer longer opening hours.||No expert knowledge is expected when you join. We give all new volunteers background information about the displays, although many gradually develop a special interest. At present, visitors most often ask questions about our section on [10]railway history[/10], as many local families had relatives employed at the old station. Volunteers do not have to give detailed talks. In their first month, they usually help with a [11]visitor survey[/11], asking people what they enjoyed and which displays were easy to understand. This helps new volunteers become confident speaking to the public without needing to memorise facts.||On duty, you should look smart but do not need a uniform. The museum supplies every volunteer with a [12]name badge[/12], which visitors find reassuring when asking for assistance. We have recently introduced an [13]audio guide[/13] for people who want to explore independently, and volunteers show visitors how to use it. It is available in several languages and includes recordings from people who remember the town in the past. Many volunteers especially enjoy helping [14]school groups[/14], whose visits include object-handling activities rather than simply walking around the galleries. Training for these visits is offered once a term.||Behind the public galleries is an important collection which is rarely seen. Old letters, maps and newspapers are kept in the [15]archive room[/15], where temperature and light are carefully controlled. Volunteers who have completed initial training may help identify items and add information to our computer records. We do not ask them to repair old photographs; instead, they learn to use a [16]photograph scanner[/16] so digital copies can be studied without damaging originals. This work is quiet but extremely valuable, and it suits people who prefer not to stand in a busy gallery all day.||Volunteering is unpaid, although travel costs within the town can be refunded. Students who work with us regularly for at least one term may ask for a [17]reference letter[/17] when applying for courses or jobs. There is also an urgent reason for recruiting more supporters this year. Our exhibition rooms are fine, but heavy rain has caused damage at the top of the building. Money from our summer open evening will be used for [18]roof repairs[/18]. Volunteers will help run activities and sell refreshments on that evening. Application forms are on your chairs, and I look forward to meeting some of you again.

dialogue.speaker: alice
dialogue.text: Hello. I’m Alice, the education officer at Barton Town Museum, and I would like to tell you how volunteers can become involved. Our museum is in a rather unusual building. Some visitors remember it as a bank, because there is still a safe downstairs, but its original purpose was as the town’s post office. It opened as a museum twenty-five years ago and now receives thousands of visitors annually. We display local objects, photographs and recorded stories, and our volunteers make it possible for us to offer longer opening hours.||No expert knowledge is expected when you join. We give all new volunteers background information about the displays, although many gradually develop a special interest. At present, visitors most often ask questions about our section on railway history, as many local families had relatives employed at the old station. Volunteers do not have to give detailed talks. In their first month, they usually help with a visitor survey, asking people what they enjoyed and which displays were easy to understand. This helps new volunteers become confident speaking to the public without needing to memorise facts.||On duty, you should look smart but do not need a uniform. The museum supplies every volunteer with a name badge, which visitors find reassuring when asking for assistance. We have recently introduced an audio guide for people who want to explore independently, and volunteers show visitors how to use it. It is available in several languages and includes recordings from people who remember the town in the past. Many volunteers especially enjoy helping school groups, whose visits include object-handling activities rather than simply walking around the galleries. Training for these visits is offered once a term.||Behind the public galleries is an important collection which is rarely seen. Old letters, maps and newspapers are kept in the archive room, where temperature and light are carefully controlled. Volunteers who have completed initial training may help identify items and add information to our computer records. We do not ask them to repair old photographs; instead, they learn to use a photograph scanner so digital copies can be studied without damaging originals. This work is quiet but extremely valuable, and it suits people who prefer not to stand in a busy gallery all day.||Volunteering is unpaid, although travel costs within the town can be refunded. Students who work with us regularly for at least one term may ask for a reference letter when applying for courses or jobs. There is also an urgent reason for recruiting more supporters this year. Our exhibition rooms are fine, but heavy rain has caused damage at the top of the building. Money from our summer open evening will be used for roof repairs. Volunteers will help run activities and sell refreshments on that evening. Application forms are on your chairs, and I look forward to meeting some of you again.

questions.question: The museum building was originally the town's (9) .
questions.explanation: Alice says the building was originally the town's 'post office'.

questions.question: Visitors frequently ask about the display on (10) .
questions.explanation: Alice identifies 'railway history' as a popular subject for visitors' questions.

questions.question: During their first month, volunteers generally help with a (11) .
questions.explanation: Alice says new volunteers usually help with a 'visitor survey'.

questions.question: Each volunteer is provided with a (12) to wear while working.
questions.explanation: Alice explains that volunteers are supplied with a 'name badge'.

questions.question: Volunteers teach visitors to operate the new (13) .
questions.explanation: Alice says volunteers show visitors how to use the 'audio guide'.

questions.question: Many volunteers particularly like working with (14) .
questions.explanation: Alice says many volunteers enjoy helping 'school groups'.

questions.question: Old written material is stored in the (15) .
questions.explanation: Alice identifies the 'archive room' as the storage location.

questions.question: Volunteers can learn how to use a (16) for old images.
questions.explanation: Alice says volunteers use a 'photograph scanner' to digitise images.

questions.question: Regular student volunteers may request a (17) .
questions.explanation: Alice offers a 'reference letter' to students who work regularly.

questions.question: Funds raised at the open evening will pay for (18) .
questions.explanation: Alice explains that money will support 'roof repairs'.
